elements.sent-2411-001: The recent development of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has led to great interest in 4D dynamic spatial reconstruction from multi-view visual inputs.
elements.sent-2411-002: While existing approaches mainly rely on processing full-length multi-view videos for 4D reconstruction, there has been limited exploration of iterative online reconstruction methods that enable on-the-fly training and per-frame streaming.
elements.sent-2411-003: Current 3DGS-based streaming methods treat the Gaussian primitives uniformly and constantly renew the densified Gaussians, thereby overlooking the difference between dynamic and static features and also neglecting the temporal continuity in the scene.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address these limitations, we propose a novel three-stage pipeline for iterative streamable 4D dynamic spatial reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our pipeline comprises a selective inheritance stage to preserve temporal continuity, a dynamics-aware shift stage for distinguishing dynamic and static primitives and optimizing their movements, and an error-guided densification stage to accommodate emerging objects.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in online 4D reconstruction, demonstrating a 20\% improvement in on-the-fly training speed, superior representation quality, and real-time rendering capability.. Project page: \url{https://www.liuzhening.top/DASS.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{Figs/time_PSNR_1115.pdf} \caption{Performance of 4D dynamic spatial reconstruction methods in terms of per-frame training time and reconstruction quality (PSNR) on the N3DV dataset~\cite{li2022neural}. Online methods are represented by circles, while offline methods are indicated by triangles. The size of each marker is proportional to the rendering speed (FPS). Our method achieves the fastest online training speed, superior reconstruction quality, and real-time rendering performance.} \label{fig:time-psnr-fps}
elements.sent-2411-007: The rapid advancements of stereoscopic cameras and rendering techniques have expanded human visual perception from 2D planes to spatial 3D representations.
elements.sent-2411-008: This evolution has paved the way for 4D dynamic free-viewpoint video (FVV) reconstruction by integrating the temporal dimension, which unlocks substantial potential for a wide range of applications, including augmented/virtual reality (AR/VR)~\cite{arena2022overview and holographic communications~\cite{tu2024tele .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Nevertheless, constructing 4D dynamic FVVs from multi-view 2D inputs remains a significant challenge.
elements.sent-2411-010: In recent years, Neural Radiance Field (NeRF)~\cite{nerf has emerged as a promising approach for spatial representation and reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-011: NeRFs optimize neural networks to estimate color and density based on spatial position and viewpoint for 3D reconstruction using captured multi-view inputs.
elements.sent-2411-012: The extensions of NeRFs to dynamic scene reconstruction have demonstrated significant effectiveness~\cite{li2022neural,DNERF,song2023nerfplayer,fridovich2023k,guo2024motion , yielding photo-realistic novel view synthesis results.}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, the efficiency of NeRF-based methods is severely hindered by their low rendering speed due to the dense queries of neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-014: To address this issue, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)~\cite{kerbl20233d has been proposed as a solution to provide high-quality reconstruction and real-time rendering capabilities, leveraging its flexible point-based primitive design and tile-based differentiable rasterization.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Subsequent research efforts~\cite{katsumata2024compact,yangreal,li2024spacetime,wu20244d,bae2024per have been dedicated to applying Gaussian Splatting for 4D dynamic reconstruction, with representative works integrating the time dimension into each Gaussian primitive~\cite{yangreal or learning spatio-temporal deformations~\cite{li2024spacetime,wu20244d .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Despite these advancements, most NeRF-based and 3DGS-based methods for dynamic spatial reconstruction rely on full-length multi-view videos, i.e., non-causal inputs.
elements.sent-2411-017: This reliance overlooks applications such as live streaming, where only per-frame causal inputs are available and on-the-fly training is required.
elements.sent-2411-018: This scenario is formalized as iteratively reconstructing 3D space at the current frame based on previous reconstruction caches and current multi-view inputs.
elements.sent-2411-019: The key challenges in this context are two-fold: (i) how to model temporal variations between frames in 3D space and (2) how to facilitate the optimization convergence from the previous frame to the current one.
elements.sent-2411-020: Critical metrics in this scenario include both the quality of novel view synthesis and streaming time efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-021: One intuitive solution is to directly optimize a new set of 3DGS primitives for each frame.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, tuning and storing all 3DGS parameters for each frame results in significant time costs and storage overhead.
elements.sent-2411-023: A representative baseline, 3DGStream~\cite{sun20243dgstream , efficiently optimizes the transformation of Gaussian positions and rotation quaternions, and adaptively densifies a small number of new Gaussians.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Although this method achieves fast and high-quality results, it overlooks the difference between inherent dynamics and statics in the scene, instead treating the whole scene uniformly.
elements.sent-2411-025: When modeling movements in the space, dynamic and static components showcase different deformation characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-026: For instance, moving objects, such as humans or animals, may display substantial dynamics, with the Gaussian properties like position experiencing significant offsets.
elements.sent-2411-027: In contrast, static background and stationary objects show minimal movement, where Gaussians remain unchanged or undergo slight jitters.
elements.sent-2411-028: Besides, in most natural scenes, only a small subset of Gaussian primitives corresponds to dynamic areas.
elements.sent-2411-029: Consequently, uniformly modeling the transformation of all Gaussians results in a sub-optimal solution.
elements.sent-2411-030: Moreover, renewing the added Gaussian primitives for each frame fails to fully exploit the temporal continuity.
elements.sent-2411-031: Based on these insights, we propose a dynamics-aware 3DGS streaming paradigm for on-the-fly 4D reconstruction, termed DASS, where the optimization of each frame comprises three stages: inheritance, shift, and densification.
elements.sent-2411-032: Specifically, considering the temporal continuity, the newly added Gaussians in the previous frame are likely to persist in subsequent frames.
elements.sent-2411-033: Therefore, instead of renewing these added Gaussians for each frame and optimizing them from scratch, we propose a selective inheritance mechanism to adaptively include a portion of the added Gaussians from the previous frame using a learnable selection mask.
elements.sent-2411-034: Then, in the shift stage, we employ 2D dynamics-related prior optical flow~\cite{xu2022gmflow,xu2023unifying,wang2025sea and Gaussian segmentation~\cite{ye2025gaussian,shen2025flashsplat to calculate a per-Gaussian dynamics mask.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Subsequently, we assign grid-based layers to learn the offsets of dynamic and static Gaussians with different representation complexities.
elements.sent-2411-036: In the densification stage, apart from the Gaussian offsets that present the deformations of existing objects, new Gaussian primitives are introduced to accommodate newly emerging objects.
elements.sent-2411-037: In this stage, both positional gradients and error maps from the shift stage serve as criteria for identifying regions that require densification.
elements.sent-2411-038: The inheritance stage in the subsequent frame will process these added Gaussians, thereby mitigating errors in the shift stage and reducing the optimization burden in the densification stage.
elements.sent-2411-039: This three-stage pipeline effectively captures dynamic spatial components and exploits the temporal correlation, providing fast on-the-fly training and high-fidelity streaming.
elements.sent-2411-040: Our main contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-041: We propose a novel three-stage pipeline for 4D dynamic spatial reconstruction that supports on-the-fly training and per-frame streaming.
elements.sent-2411-042: Our method builds on causal inputs and eliminates the need for full-length multi-view videos, thereby enhancing the practicability.
elements.sent-2411-043: Our approach seamlessly integrates the three stages to optimize the reconstruction quality.
elements.sent-2411-044: By selectively inheriting newly introduced Gaussians from the preceding frame, effectively distinguishing dynamic primitives to allocate optimization emphasis, and enhancing areas with weak reconstruction using gradient information and optimization errors, our method ensures high-fidelity dynamic spatial reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-045: Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of our method in multiple aspects, including a 20\% improvement in online training speed, superior reconstruction quality, and real-time rendering capability.
elements.sent-2411-046: In recent years, reconstructing 3D representations from 2D plane visual inputs has experienced significant advancements, driven by the development of NeRF~\cite{nerf .}
elements.sent-2411-047: NeRF-based methods represent spatial scenes by optimizing multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) and generate novel views through volume rendering~\cite{kajiya1984ray .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Subsequent research has enhanced both the training and rendering efficiency through grid-based designs~\cite{muller2022instant,xu2022point,fridovich2022plenoxels,fridovich2023k .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Nonetheless, NeRF-based approaches typically require dense ray tracing and struggle to fulfill high-speed rendering.
elements.sent-2411-050: Recently, 3DGS~\cite{kerbl20233d has emerged to address these limitations by utilizing explicit unstructured scene representation while preserving point-based differentiable splatting rendering~\cite{zwicker2001ewa .}}
elements.sent-2411-051: This approach achieves real-time rendering speed and photo-realistic quality.
elements.sent-2411-052: Based on these advancements, subsequent studies have further enhanced the representation efficiency~\cite{lu2024scaffold,chen2024hac,liu2024compgs,fan2023lightgaussian,lee2024compact,zhang2025gaussianimage , developed feed-forward reconstruction models~\cite{zheng2024gps,liu2025mvsgaussian,charatan2024pixelsplat,hu2024eva , and expanded applications in understanding and editing~\cite{ye2025gaussian,chen2024gaussianeditor,shen2025flashsplat,wang2024gscream .}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Extending static scene representation to dynamic FVV reconstruction remains a significant challenge, primarily due to the difficulties in modeling temporal correlations and variations in 3D space.
elements.sent-2411-054: To address this issue, several studies have extended NeRF to incorporate spatio-temporal structures~\cite{li2022streaming,cao2023hexplane,song2023nerfplayer,fridovich2023k,wang2023mixed , facilitating dynamic space reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Similarly, dynamic scene reconstruction methods based on Gaussian Splatting have been proposed, which can be categorized into deformation-based methods, 4D primitive-based methods, and iterative streaming methods.
elements.sent-2411-056: Deformation-based methods~\cite{wu20244d,lu20243d,yang2024deformable,bae2024per maintain 3D Gaussian representations and optimize a neural deformable field to capture temporal variations.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Note that both categories of methods rely on full-length multi-view videos for training, which limits their ability to support on-the-fly training and per-frame streaming.
elements.sent-2411-058: In contrast, iterative streaming methods build upon previously converged representations and perform per-frame optimization for each frame's multi-view inputs, thereby addressing the above limitations.
elements.sent-2411-059: However, this area remains largely underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-060: Our work aims to develop an iterative streaming pipeline that accelerates per-frame optimization convergence and enhances the reconstruction quality.
elements.sent-2411-061: The work most closely related to ours is 3DGStream~\cite{sun20243dgstream .}
elements.sent-2411-062: It proposes a two-stage optimization process for each frame, where the first stage optimizes a grid-based MLP~\cite{muller2022instant for Gaussian property transformation and the second stage wisely adds new Gaussians based on positional gradients.}
elements.sent-2411-063: However, 3DGStream does not consider the intrinsic dynamic and static features in the scene and erases the added primitives in each frame, which neglects the temporal consistency.
elements.sent-2411-064: We have also noticed several concurrent studies on iterative 4D reconstruction, although their focuses differ from ours.
elements.sent-2411-065: For instance, S4D~\cite{he2024s4d directly manipulates 3D control points to guide the movements, but it requires full optimization that incurs significant time costs on key frames.}
elements.sent-2411-066: V$^3$~\cite{wang2024v aims to facilitate streaming free-viewpoint videos on mobile devices, with a particular emphasis on the reduction of the per-frame streaming storage overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Conversely, our work identifies the per-frame convergence speed as the critical bottleneck.
elements.sent-2411-068: Moreover, while V$^3$ decomposes motion and appearance attributes for efficient reconstruction, it primarily applies to scenarios with moving objects against vacant background and does not accommodate emerging objects.
elements.sent-2411-069: In addition, SwinGS~\cite{liu2024swings concentrates on maintaining a consistently stable stream data volume over time and reducing storage overhead through the use of Markov Chain Monte Carlo~\cite{kheradmand20243d and a window-based design.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Another work~\cite{sun2024multi considers the bit allocation in a system-level implementation.}
elements.sent-2411-071: In contrast, our work focuses on achieving fast convergence in per-frame optimization while maintaining high reconstruction quality, which is orthogonal to the contributions of aforementioned studies.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.935\linewidth]{Figs/pipeline_vert_cut.pdf} \caption{Overview of our proposed DASS framework. The selective inheritance stage (Green) exploits the temporal continuity and adaptively preserves Gaussians from the previous frame. The dynamics-aware shift stage (Blue) distinguishes the dynamic and static elements and optimizes the deformations. The error-guided densification stage (Yellow) detects and densifies the areas with weak reconstruction based on positional gradients and distortions. Variables highlighted in red represent learnable parameters in each stage, whose training is significantly lightweight compared to tuning all Gaussian parameters.} \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-072: Our method, referred to as DASS, achieves 4D dynamic spatial reconstruction in an iterative manner, enabling on-the-fly training and per-frame streaming.
elements.sent-2411-073: At each timestep $t$, the optimization begins with the representation of the previous timestep, denoted by $\bm{\mathcal{G _{t-1}$, to derive the current representation $\bm{\mathcal{G _{t}$, based on the current multi-view inputs from $C$ viewpoints.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: For each scene, the pipeline starts from an initial set of 3D Gaussians at frame 0.
elements.sent-2411-075: This initial representation $\bm{\mathcal{G _{0}$ consistently serves as a base representation $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{base}$ with a constant number of primitives $N$ across subsequent frames.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Moreover, each timestep incorporates a varying yet limited number of densified Gaussians, denoted by $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{densi}$, to model the unique per-frame objects.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: A subset of these densified Gaussians is inherited to the next timestep as $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{inher}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Fig. \ref{fig:pipeline depicts an overview of our DASS framework, where the optimization for each frame comprises three stages: inheritance, shift, and densification.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Unlike previous methods that regenerate densified Gaussians at each timestep without considering temporal continuity, our approach introduces a selective inheritance stage (Sec.
elements.sent-2411-080: During this stage, the densified Gaussians from the preceding timestep, $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{densi _{t-1}$, are selectively inherited using a per-Gaussian learnable mask.}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: This mask effectively eliminates redundant Gaussians while preserving essential ones, transforming the set from $\{\bm{\mathcal{G ^{base _{t-1 ,\bm{\mathcal{G ^{densi _{t-1}\}$ to $\{\bm{\mathcal{G ^{base _{t-1 ,\bm{\mathcal{G ^{inher _{t-1}\}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: In this stage, a Gaussian-level dynamics mask is estimated to categorize the Gaussians into dynamic and static groups.
elements.sent-2411-084: These categories are then processed by two hash-encoding MLPs that operate at different complexities.
elements.sent-2411-085: This shift stage efficiently manages the movements and rotations of Gaussian primitives, accommodating changes from the previous timestep and updating the representation as $\{\bm{\mathcal{G ^{base _{t-1 ,\bm{\mathcal{G ^{inher _{t-1}\}\to\{\bm{\mathcal{G ^{base _{t ,\bm{\mathcal{G ^{inher _{t}\}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Then, this stage densifies additional Gaussian primitives to accurately represent these emerging objects and yields $\{\bm{\mathcal{G ^{base _{t ,\bm{\mathcal{G ^{inher _{t}\}\to\{\bm{\mathcal{G ^{base _{t ,\bm{\mathcal{G ^{densi _{t}\}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Our proposed design facilitates fast per-frame convergence and produces high-fidelity view synthesis results.
elements.sent-2411-089: The details of each stage are elaborated in the following subsections.
elements.sent-2411-090: In our pipeline, the shift and densification stages focus on the deformation of existing objects and the densification for emerging objects, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-091: Due to the strong temporal consistency inherent in the scene, the densified Gaussians from the previous frame, $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{densi _{t-1}$, are likely to persist and remain valid in the current frame, since they have been specifically optimized for the scene.}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Leveraging these frame-wise priors and dependencies for subsequent frames can significantly accelerate on-the-fly training convergence.
elements.sent-2411-093: However, previous methods, such as 3DGStream~\cite{sun20243dgstream , typically avoid reusing these Gaussians to prevent the accumulation of an excessive number of Gaussians, which can lead to prohibitive training and streaming overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-094: To address this limitation, we propose a selective inheritance mechanism for the densified Gaussian primitives $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{densi _{t-1}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: This approach adaptively selects a subset of the Gaussians that are beneficial for the reconstruction while controlling the total number of Gaussians to avoid excessive accumulation.
elements.sent-2411-096: Specifically, for each Gaussian primitive in $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{densi _{t-1}$, we assign a learnable parameter.}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: The parameter vector for these Gaussians is denoted by $\mathbf{m}$.
elements.sent-2411-098: We then apply the sigmoid function to $\mathbf{m}$, yielding $sigmoid(\mathbf{m )$, followed by quantization to generate a binary mask.}
elements.sent-2411-099: This mask indicates the existence of inherited Gaussians, which is element-wise multiplied with the Gaussian opacities and scales as follows: \begin{equation}\setlength\abovedisplayskip{3pt} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{2pt} \begin{aligned} \mathbf{o}_r =Quant(sigmoid(\mathbf{m}))\circ \mathbf{o}, \mathbf{s}_r =Quant(sigmoid(\mathbf{m}))\circ \mathbf{s}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{o _r$ and $\mathbf{s _r$ are the opacity and scale values used during rendering, $\mathbf{o}$ and $\mathbf{s}$ are the original opacity and scale values, and $\circ$ represents the element-wise multiplication.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: When the mask is quantized to zero, the corresponding Gaussian has zero opacity and scale, thus contributing nothing to the rendering process.
elements.sent-2411-101: Therefore, this mask determines whether each Gaussian is retained in the rendering.
elements.sent-2411-102: By optimizing $\mathbf{m}$, the method selectively inherits the most relevant densified Gaussians from $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{densi _{t-1}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: The loss function for optimizing $\mathbf{m}$ is expressed as follows: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}=(1-\lambda)\mathcal{L}_1+\lambda\mathcal{L}_{D-SSIM}+\lambda_{inher}\sumsigmoid(\mathbf{m}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-104: The first two terms represent the fidelity loss in the vanilla 3DGS~\cite{kerbl20233d , where $\mathcal{L _1$ and $\mathcal{L _{D-SSIM}$ are pixel-level $L_1$ loss and D-SSIM~\cite{wang2003multiscale loss, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: The final term, weighted by $\lambda_{inher}$, is referred to as the mask loss and serves as a regularizer that encourages the parameters in $\mathbf{m}$ to approach zero.
elements.sent-2411-106: This regularization effectively reduces the number of inherited Gaussians and controls the numerical accumulation, while the fidelity loss aims to keep the Gaussians that are beneficial for reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-107: Consequently, this trade-off optimizes the learnable $\mathbf{m}$ and selectively inherits important Gaussians from $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{densi _{t-1}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: After $s_1$ optimization steps, the final optimized mask $Quant(sigmoid(\mathbf{m ))$ is employed to remove redundant densified Gaussians and preserve the important ones as $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{inher _{t-1}$, which is subsequently fed into the following shift and densification stages.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: This selective inheritance enhances the optimization pipeline in two key aspects.
elements.sent-2411-110: First, when training at timestep $t$ with the previous timestep's base Gaussians $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{base _{t-1}$, distortions may occur due to temporal transformations and emerging objects.}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Feeding the base Gaussians directly into the shift stage can lead to inaccurate adjustments in Gaussian properties.
elements.sent-2411-112: Selectively inheriting the previous densified Gaussians $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{inher _{t-1}$ before the shift stage mitigates these optimization errors.}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: Second, after the selective inheritance and shift stages, we obtain a refined set of optimized Gaussians $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{inher _{t}$, which reduces the number of densified Gaussians requiring optimization, thus improving training efficiency.}}}

elements.sent-2411-114: In this stage, we focus on modeling the movements and rotations of Gaussian primitives from the preceding frame to the current one.
elements.sent-2411-115: A common approach to achieve this is directly learning deformation fields to accommodate temporal transformations, as in previous deformation-based method~\cite{wu20244d and iterative streaming method~\cite{sun20243dgstream .}}
elements.sent-2411-116: However, these approaches often overlook the significant diversity in the movements of Gaussian primitives present in natural scenes, which can result in slower convergence.
elements.sent-2411-117: Specifically, Gaussians representing background or stationary object typically show high similarity with minimal variations or remain unchanged, while those in the foreground, such as humans, animals, and other moving objects, display significant dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-118: This disparity is illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:flame_steak_hist , which presents a histogram of Gaussian positional offsets between frames in the flame steak scene of N3DV~\cite{li2022neural .}}
elements.sent-2411-119: We observe that most offsets fall within the range of $(0, 0.01]$, which indicates that the majority of Gaussians undergo only minor shifts.
elements.sent-2411-120: Therefore, it is inappropriate to optimize the diverse variations in the scene using a single deformation field.
elements.sent-2411-121: To address this, we propose estimating a per-Gaussian dynamics mask for $\bm{\mathcal{G _{t-1 =\{\bm{\mathcal{G ^{base _{t-1 ,\bm{\mathcal{G ^{inher _{t-1}\}$ before deformation.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: This mask is used to categorize all primitives into dynamic and static groups.
elements.sent-2411-123: Then, we assign two deformation layers to learn the respective Gaussian transformations: A complex hash-encoding MLP~\cite{muller2022instant to capture the intricate shifts and rotations in the dynamic group, and a simpler hash-encoding MLP to model the regionally similar minor variations in the static group.}

The process begins with optical flow and segmentation, which provide 2D priors to identify the dynamic object IDs. These IDs are then used to find the corresponding Gaussians in the 3D space.} \label{fig:dyna_mask}
elements.sent-2411-124: To construct the per-Gaussian dynamics mask, we leverage established techniques from optical flow~\cite{xu2022gmflow,xu2023unifying,wang2025sea and segmentation~\cite{kirillov2023segment,ke2024segment,ye2025gaussian,shen2025flashsplat .}}
elements.sent-2411-125: Specifically, we utilize Gaussian Grouping~\cite{ye2025gaussian to initialize the scene $\bm{\mathcal{G _0$, which assigns an object property, akin to color property, to each Gaussian primitive and optimizes this property with 2D segmentation results serving as ground truth.}}}
elements.sent-2411-126: These object properties provide per-Gaussian segmentation results that connect 2D images with 3D Gaussians, which facilitates the rendering of segmentation results on any viewpoint.
elements.sent-2411-127: Notably, these object properties remain fixed in subsequent frames $\bm{\mathcal{G _t (t>0)$, thus posing no additional burden on the time efficiency of on-the-fly training.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: For subsequent frames, we infer an optical flow estimation network~\cite{xu2023unifying from consecutive frames captured from the same viewpoint and identify the areas where the optical flow exceeds a threshold $\gamma_{op}$, which we classify as dynamic areas.}
elements.sent-2411-129: We then query the rendered segmentation results to find out the dynamic object IDs.
elements.sent-2411-130: The dynamic Gaussians are consistently detected using these IDs, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:dyna_mask .}
elements.sent-2411-131: This process utilizes off-the-shelf methods that do not require network training, allowing us to obtain the per-Gaussian dynamics mask within milliseconds (about 300 ms).
elements.sent-2411-132: An introduction of these methods is provided in the supplementary material.
elements.sent-2411-133: After obtaining the dynamics mask, we employ the multi-resolution hash-encoding layer I-NGP~\cite{muller2022instant to learn the deformations.}
elements.sent-2411-134: For each Gaussian, the deformation includes a position offset $\bm{\mu _n\in \mathbb{R ^{3}$, which is added to the Gaussian position $\bm{p _n$, and a rotation offset $\bm{\sigma _n\in \mathbb{R ^4$, which is applied to the Gaussian rotation quaternion $\bm{q _n$ by using $\mathrm{norm ({\bm{q _n )\times\mathrm{norm (\bm{\sigma _n)$.}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-137: For the dynamic group, we utilize a large-sized hash-encoding layer $\mathcal{H _{dyn}$ with $T_{Hash = T_{large}$ and $F_{Hash = F_{large}$ to accommodate the complex variations of dynamic Gaussians.}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: In contrast, for the static group, we employ a simpler hash-encoding layer $\mathcal{H _{st}$ with $T_{Hash =T_{large /4$ and $F_{Hash =F_{large /2$, conserving computational resources while effectively managing minor jitters in background and stationary objects.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: These lightweight networks are optimized and provide a more efficient alternative to directly tuning the parameters of all Gaussians.

elements.sent-2411-141: The different deformation patterns observed in the dynamic and static groups validate the effectiveness of our proposed strategy.

elements.sent-2411-143: However, similar to the vanilla 3DGS~\cite{kerbl20233d and 4DGS~\cite{yangreal , densification remains essential to address areas with insufficient reconstruction quality and new objects.}}
elements.sent-2411-144: To achieve fast and high-quality densification, it is crucial to identify the subset of Gaussians in need of densification.
elements.sent-2411-145: Previous methods~\cite{kerbl20233d,sun20243dgstream detect these under-reconstructed Gaussians based on historical view-space positional gradients, denoted by $\overline{\nabla \mathbf{p \triangleq \{\overline{\nabla p_n , \forall n\} \in \mathbb{R ^{N\times 1}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-146: This approach tracks gradients from previous training steps and densifies Gaussians whose positional gradients exceed a specific threshold $\tau_{pos}$.
elements.sent-2411-147: While this criterion effectively identifies general regions with inadequate reconstruction, it does not provide sufficient emphasis on regions with emerging objects or significant reconstruction errors.
elements.sent-2411-148: This limitation can hinder the efficient optimization of emerging objects, potentially slowing the convergence of on-the-fly training.
elements.sent-2411-149: To address this, we propose an error-adaptive densification strategy that incorporates an additional indicator to identify Gaussians that require densification.
elements.sent-2411-150: This approach projects high-distortion areas from the image space to the 3D space and adaptively enhances densification in critical areas.
elements.sent-2411-151: Specifically, we collect 2D error maps by comparing the ground truth with the rendered results in the shift stage for each training viewpoint, denoted by $\mathbf{E ^c$, $c=1, 2, \cdots, C$.}
elements.sent-2411-152: Then, we filter out the pixel positions with severe distortion above $\gamma_{err}$, yielding an $H\times W$ binary matrix $\mathbf{D _{err ^c=\{\mathbf{1 [e_i^c>\gamma_{err ],\forall e^c_i\in \mathbf{E ^c, i = 1, \cdots, H \times W \}$ in the 2D pixel plane, where $\mathbf{1 [\cdot]$ is the binary indicator function.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-163: Our proposed pipeline, DASS, effectively achieves iterative streamable 4D reconstruction through the seamless integration of the above three stages.
elements.sent-2411-164: The inheritance stage selectively preserves the optimized densified Gaussians from the previous frame.
elements.sent-2411-165: By exploiting the temporal consistency, this stage compensates for distortions caused by emerging objects before the shift stage and alleviates the optimization burden in the subsequent densification stage.
elements.sent-2411-166: The dynamics-aware shift stage incorporates separate deformation fields for dynamic and static features.
elements.sent-2411-167: This design is tailored to accommodate complex movements in the dynamic elements while conversing optimization resources for static components.
elements.sent-2411-168: After solving the temporal deformations, the error-guided densification stage reconstructs emerging objects in the current frame.
elements.sent-2411-169: It employs both positional gradients and rendering distortions as references to effectively identify the areas with weak reconstruction and emerging elements.
elements.sent-2411-170: Collectively, these three stages enable DASS to achieve high-fidelity reconstruction while significantly accelerating on-the-fly optimization.

elements.sent-2411-224: In this paper, we introduce a novel three-stage pipeline for iterative online 4D dynamic spatial reconstruction, which allows for on-the-fly training and per-frame streaming.
elements.sent-2411-225: We incorporate a selective inheritance stage to capture the temporal continuity, a dynamics-aware shift stage to exploit the dynamic and static features in natural scenes, and an error-guided densification stage to adaptively recover emerging objects and weak reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-226: Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in online streaming 4D reconstruction, providing the fastest training speed, with a 20\% improvement, and superior representation quality.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-227: A Gaussian primitive is expressed as: \begin{equation}G(\bm{x}) = e^{-\frac{1}{2}(\bm{x}-\bm{\mu})^T \bm{\Sigma}^{-1} (\bm{x}-\bm{\mu})}, \label{3dgs}\end{equation} where $\bm{\mu \in \mathbb{R ^3$ and $\bm{\Sigma \in \mathbb{R ^{3 \times 3}$ denote the position vector and the covariance matrix, respectively.}}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \renewcommand{\algorithmicrequire}{Input:} \renewcommand{\algorithmicensure}{Output:} \caption{Projection of 3D Gaussian positions to 2D image plane pixels} \label{alg:projection} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Positions of $N$ 3D Gaussians $\mathbf{P}=[\mathbf{X},\mathbf{Y},\mathbf{Z}]\in \mathbb{R}^{N\times 3}$, rendered image size $H$ and $W$, full projection matrix $\mathbf{T}\in \mathbb{R}^{4\times 4}$ embedded in the 3DGS camera model, which is composed of the camera intrinsic matrix and the extrinsic matrix. \STATE Calculate the projection in homography space as $\mathbf{P}_{hom}=[\mathbf{P},\mathbf{1}]\cdot \mathbf{T}=[\mathbf{p}_{x},\mathbf{p}_{y},\mathbf{p}_{z},\mathbf{p}_{hom}]$. \STATE Normalize the coordinates on the image plane as $\mathbf{x}_{norm}=\mathbf{p}_{x}/\mathbf{p}_{hom}$ and $\mathbf{y}_{norm}=\mathbf{p}_{y}/\mathbf{p}_{hom}$ \STATE Obtain the corresponding pixel-level coordinates as $\mathbf{x}_n=Round(0.5\cdot((\mathbf{x}_{norm}+1)\cdot$W$-1))$ and $\mathbf{y}_n=Round(0.5\cdot((\mathbf{x}_{norm}+1)\cdot $H$-1))$ \ENSURE The corresponding pixels of the 3D Gaussian positions as $(\mathbf{X},\mathbf{Y})=[\mathbf{x}_n,\mathbf{y}_n]$. \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-305: Consequently, this approach enhances the computational efficiency and reduces communication overhead, which are critical requirements in current 2D online meeting applications.
elements.sent-2411-306: Besides, the error-guided densification proves effective in rapidly compensating for areas with weak reconstruction.

elements.sent-2411-308: In the aspect of performance improvement, similar to other iterative streamable 4D reconstruction methods, our DASS consistently benefits from the prospective higher quality of initialization, faster rasterization, and improved training strategies in the 3DGS field, as well as frame-wise and batch-wise parallel computation.
elements.sent-2411-309: For future extensions of DASS, we plan to consider two key areas: fast on-the-fly training under sparse viewpoint inputs and adaptation to significant scene changes, which have been long-standing challenges in spatial representation tasks.

elements.sent-2411-314: The recent development of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has led to great interest in 4D dynamic spatial reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-315: Existing approaches mainly rely on full-length multi-view videos, while there has been limited exploration of online reconstruction methods that enable on-the-fly training and per-timestep streaming.
elements.sent-2411-316: Current 3DGS-based streaming methods treat the Gaussian primitives uniformly and constantly renew the densified Gaussians, thereby overlooking the difference between dynamic and static features as well as neglecting the temporal continuity in the scene.
elements.sent-2411-317: Our pipeline comprises a selective inheritance stage to preserve temporal continuity, a dynamics-aware shift stage to distinguish dynamic and static primitives and optimize their movements, and an error-guided densification stage to accommodate emerging objects.
elements.sent-2411-318: Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in online 4D reconstruction, demonstrating the fastest on-the-fly training, superior representation quality, and real-time rendering capability.. Project page: \url{https://www.liuzhening.top/DASS.}
elements.sent-2411-319: In recent years, Neural Radiance Field (NeRF)~\cite{nerf has emerged as a promising approach for spatial representation from multi-view inputs, which optimizes neural networks to estimate color and density based on spatial positions and viewpoints.}
elements.sent-2411-320: The extensions of NeRF to dynamic scene reconstruction have demonstrated remarkable effectiveness~\cite{li2022neural,DNERF,song2023nerfplayer,fridovich2023k,guo2024motion , yielding photo-realistic novel view synthesis.}
elements.sent-2411-321: Subsequent efforts~\cite{katsumata2024compact,yangreal,li2024spacetime,wu20244d,bae2024per have been dedicated to applying Gaussian Splatting for 4D dynamic reconstruction, with representative works integrating the time dimension into each Gaussian primitive~\cite{yangreal or learning spatio-temporal deformations~\cite{li2024spacetime,wu20244d .}}}
elements.sent-2411-322: Despite these advancements, most NeRF-based and 3DGS-based methods for 4D dynamic spatial reconstruction rely on full-length multi-view video inputs, which are non-causal in nature.
elements.sent-2411-323: This reliance limits their applicability in scenarios such as live streaming, where only per-timestep causal inputs are available, necessitating on-the-fly training.
elements.sent-2411-324: This problem can be formalized as the iterative reconstruction of 3D space at the current timestep, leveraging the reconstruction caches from the previous timestep and the multi-view inputs of the current timestep.
elements.sent-2411-325: The key challenges in this context are two-fold: (i) how to effectively model temporal variations in 3D space across consecutive timesteps and (ii) how to facilitate efficient optimization convergence from the previous timestep to the current one.
elements.sent-2411-326: Critical performance metrics for this problem include both the quality of novel view synthesis and the time efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-327: One intuitive solution is to optimize a new set of 3DGS primitives for each timestep independently.
elements.sent-2411-328: However, this strategy incurs substantial computational and storage costs, as it requires tuning and storing all 3DGS parameters for every timestep.
elements.sent-2411-329: A representative baseline, 3DGStream~\cite{sun20243dgstream , addresses this issue by optimizing the transformation of Gaussian positions and rotation quaternions while adaptively densifying new Gaussians.}
elements.sent-2411-330: Although this method achieves fast and high-quality reconstruction, it treats the entire scene uniformly and fails to distinguish between inherent dynamics and statics in the scene.
elements.sent-2411-331: In practice, dynamic and static components have different deformation characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-332: For instance, moving objects, such as humans or animals, display substantial dynamics, with the Gaussian properties like positions experiencing significant offsets.
elements.sent-2411-333: Consequently, uniformly modeling the transformation of all Gaussians serves as a sub-optimal strategy.
elements.sent-2411-334: Moreover, renewing the added Gaussian primitives for each timestep overlooks the temporal continuity inherent in the scene, leading to inefficiencies in both computation and representation.
elements.sent-2411-335: Based on these insights, we propose a dynamics-aware 3DGS streaming paradigm for on-the-fly 4D reconstruction, termed DASS, where the optimization of each timestep comprises three stages: inheritance, shift, and densification.
elements.sent-2411-336: Specifically, considering the temporal continuity, the newly densified Gaussians in the previous timestep are likely to persist in subsequent timesteps.
elements.sent-2411-337: Therefore, instead of renewing and optimizing added Gaussians for each timestep from scratch, we propose a selective inheritance mechanism to adaptively include a portion of the added Gaussians from the previous timestep using a learnable selection mask.
elements.sent-2411-338: Then, in the shift stage, we employ 2D dynamics-related priors, optical flow~\cite{xu2023unifying and Gaussian segmentation~\cite{ye2025gaussian to calculate a per-Gaussian dynamics mask.}}
elements.sent-2411-339: Subsequently, we assign deformation layers to learn the offsets of dynamic and static Gaussians with different representation complexities, boosting a more dedicated optimization.
elements.sent-2411-340: Iteratively, the inheritance stage in the subsequent timestep will process these added Gaussians, thereby mitigating errors in the shift stage and reducing the optimization burden in the densification stage.
elements.sent-2411-341: Our three-stage pipeline effectively captures dynamic spatial components and exploits the temporal correlation, achieving fast and high-fidelity on-the-fly 4D reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-342: We propose a novel three-stage pipeline for 4D dynamic spatial reconstruction that supports on-the-fly training and per-timestep streaming.
elements.sent-2411-343: Our approach seamlessly integrates three stages to optimize reconstruction quality.
elements.sent-2411-344: By selectively inheriting Gaussians from the preceding timestep, effectively distinguishing dynamic primitives to allocate optimization emphasis, and refining under-reconstructed areas using gradient information and optimization errors, our method ensures high-fidelity dynamic spatial reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-345: Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of our method in multiple aspects, including the highest online training speed, superior reconstruction quality, and real-time rendering capability.
elements.sent-2411-346: In recent years, reconstructing 3D representations from 2D plane visual inputs has experienced significant advancements, driven by the development of NeRF~\cite{nerf and 3DGS~\cite{kerbl20233d .}}
elements.sent-2411-347: Based on these advancements, subsequent studies have further enhanced the representation efficiency~\cite{lu2024scaffold,chen2024hac,liu2024compgs,fan2023lightgaussian,lee2024compact,zhang2025gaussianimage,fan2024instantsplat,ye20253d and expanded applications in understanding and editing~\cite{ye2025gaussian,zhou2024feature,chen2024gaussianeditor,shen2025flashsplat,wang2024gscream .}}
elements.sent-2411-348: Deformation-based methods~\cite{wu20244d,lu20243d,yang2024deformable,bae2024per maintain 3D Gaussian representations and optimize neural deformable fields to capture temporal variations.}
elements.sent-2411-349: Note that both categories of methods rely on full-length multi-view videos for training, which limits their ability to support on-the-fly training and per-timestep streaming.
elements.sent-2411-350: In contrast, iterative streaming methods build upon previously converged representations and perform per-timestep optimization for each timestep's multi-view inputs, thereby addressing the above limitations.
elements.sent-2411-351: Our work aims to develop an iterative streaming pipeline that accelerates per-timestep optimization convergence and enhances the reconstruction quality.
elements.sent-2411-352: The work most related to ours is 3DGStream~\cite{sun20243dgstream .}
elements.sent-2411-353: It proposes a two-stage optimization process for each timestep, where the first stage optimizes a grid-based MLP~\cite{muller2022instant for Gaussian property transformation and the second stage wisely adds new Gaussians based on positional gradients.}
elements.sent-2411-354: However, 3DGStream does not consider the intrinsic difference between dynamic and static features in the scene and discards the added primitives at each timestep, thereby limiting its ability to leverage temporal continuity across timesteps.
elements.sent-2411-355: Concurrently, other studies on iterative 4D reconstruction focus on different aspects, such as reducing streaming storage overhead on mobile devices~\cite{wang2024v and exploring integration into communication systems~\cite{liu2024swings,sun2024multi .}}
elements.sent-2411-356: In contrast, our work focuses on achieving fast convergence in per-timestep optimization while maintaining high reconstruction quality, which is orthogonal to the contributions of aforementioned studies.
elements.figure-2411-018: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{Figs/pipeline_vert_Mar.pdf} \caption{Overview of our proposed DASS framework. Variables highlighted in red represent learnable parameters in each stage, whose training is significantly lightweight compared to tuning all Gaussian parameters.} \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-357: Our work addresses the problem of Gaussian Splatting streaming, which is formalized as an iterative 4D dynamic spatial reconstruction process, enabling on-the-fly training and per-timestep streaming.
elements.sent-2411-358: At each timestep $t$, the optimization begins with the representation from the previous timestep, denoted by $\bm{\mathcal{G _{t-1}$, and derives the current representation $\bm{\mathcal{G _{t}$, using multi-view inputs from $C$ viewpoints.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-359: Our streaming pipeline, referred to as DASS, represents the 3D space at each timestep $t$ using two sets of Gaussian primitives: quantity-consistent base Gaussians and temporally quantity-varied densified Gaussians, i.e., $\bm{\mathcal{G _{t :=\{\bm{\mathcal{G _t^{base ,\bm{\mathcal{G _t^{var}\}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-360: The base Gaussians $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{base}$, initialized from the zero timestep representation $\bm{\mathcal{G _{0}$, constitute the fundamental elements shared across all timesteps and maintain a fixed number of $N$ Gaussian primitives throughout the streaming process.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-361: In contrast, the number of Gaussians in $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{var}$ varies across timesteps, capturing per-timestep unique emerging objects that are not represented in $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{base}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-362: Fig. \ref{fig:pipeline depicts an overview of our framework, where the optimization for each timestep comprises three stages: inheritance, shift, and densification.}
elements.sent-2411-364: This mask effectively eliminates redundant Gaussians while retaining those essential for the current timestep.
elements.sent-2411-365: In this stage, a Gaussian-level dynamics mask is estimated to classify the Gaussians into dynamic and static groups, which are then processed by two hash-encoding MLPs with different complexities.
elements.sent-2411-366: This shift stage efficiently manages the movements and rotations of Gaussians, accommodating changes from the previous timestep.
elements.sent-2411-367: This stage densifies a limited number of additional Gaussians to accurately represent these emerging objects.
elements.sent-2411-368: Our proposed design facilitates fast per-timestep convergence and achieves high-fidelity novel view synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-369: Exploiting temporal correlations is critical for effective and efficient Gaussian Splatting streaming.
elements.sent-2411-370: Due to the strong temporal consistency inherent in the scene, the base Gaussians $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{base}$ and a subset of the temporally-varied Gaussians $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{var}$ from the previous timestep remain applicable for subsequent timesteps, since they have been specifically optimized for the scene.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-371: Leveraging these timestep-wise priors and dependencies can significantly accelerate on-the-fly training convergence.
elements.sent-2411-372: However, previous methods, such as 3DGStream~\cite{sun20243dgstream , typically retain the base Gaussians $\bm{\mathcal{G _{t-1 ^{base}$ while discarding the previously densified Gaussians $\bm{\mathcal{G _{t-1 ^{var}$ to prevent excessive accumulation of Gaussians, which can lead to prohibitive training and streaming overhead.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-373: To address this limitation, we propose a selective inheritance mechanism for $\bm{\mathcal{G _{t-1 ^{var}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-374: Our approach adaptively selects a subset of the Gaussians that are beneficial for the current reconstruction while controlling the total number of Gaussians to avoid excessive accumulation.
elements.sent-2411-375: Specifically, for each Gaussian primitive in $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{var _{t-1}$, we assign a learnable parameter, resulting in a parameter vector $\mathbf{m}\in\mathbb{R ^{N\times 1}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-376: This mask indicates the selection of inherited Gaussians and is element-wise multiplied with the Gaussian opacities and scales as follows: \begin{equation}\setlength\abovedisplayskip{3pt} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{2pt} \begin{aligned} \mathbf{o}_r =Quant(sigmoid(\mathbf{m}))\circ \mathbf{o}, \mathbf{s}_r =Quant(sigmoid(\mathbf{m}))\circ \mathbf{s}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{o _r$ and $\mathbf{s _r$ are the opacity and scale values used during rendering, $\mathbf{o}$ and $\mathbf{s}$ are the original opacity and scale values, and $\circ$ represents the element-wise multiplication.}}
elements.sent-2411-377: Therefore, this mask determines whether each Gaussian is retained for rendering.
elements.sent-2411-378: By optimizing $\mathbf{m}$, the method selectively inherits the most relevant densified Gaussians from $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{var _{t-1}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-379: The loss function for optimizing $\mathbf{m}$ is expressed as follows: \begin{equation}\setlength\abovedisplayskip{1pt} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{1pt} \mathcal{L}=(1-\lambda)\mathcal{L}_1+\lambda\mathcal{L}_{D-SSIM}+\lambda_{inher}\sumsigmoid(\mathbf{m}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-380: The first two terms represent the fidelity loss in the vanilla 3DGS~\cite{kerbl20233d , comprising pixel-level $L_1$ loss and D-SSIM~\cite{wang2003multiscale loss, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-381: The final term, weighted by $\lambda_{inher}$, is referred to as the mask loss and acts as a regularizer that encourages the parameters in $\mathbf{m}$ to approach zero.
elements.sent-2411-382: This regularization effectively reduces the number of inherited Gaussians and controls numerical accumulation, while the fidelity loss aims to retain Gaussians that are beneficial for reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-383: Consequently, with only a few optimization iterations, this trade-off effectively optimizes the learnable $\mathbf{m}$ and selectively inherits important Gaussians from $\bm{\mathcal{G ^{var _{t-1}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-384: The final optimized mask $Quant(sigmoid(\mathbf{m ))$ is employed to remove redundant densified Gaussians and preserve the important ones, which are then fed into the shift stage.}
elements.sent-2411-385: This selective inheritance mechanism enhances the optimization pipeline in two key aspects.
elements.sent-2411-386: First, the differences between timesteps arise from both temporal transformations and emerging objects.
elements.sent-2411-387: Directly feeding only the base Gaussians into shift stage can lead to inaccurate adjustments in Gaussian properties.

elements.sent-2411-422: We focus on fast on-the-fly training for streamable dynamic spatial reconstruction, emphasizing both time efficiency and reconstruction fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-423: To evaluate time efficiency, we calculate the average per-timestep training time across the entire video sequence and measure the frames per second (FPS).
elements.sent-2411-424: For offline methods that do not support on-the-fly training, we report their training time averaged over all timesteps.
elements.sent-2411-425: Our method achieves the fastest on-the-fly reconstruction speed, which converges from the previous timestep to the current one within 10 seconds, yielding a significant improvement in efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-426: This significant time efficiency advantage originates from several key designs: First, our inheritance stage effectively captures the temporal continuity and selectively preserves the optimized results from the previous timestep.
elements.sent-2411-427: This significantly alleviates the optimization burden in subsequent stages, especially in the densification stage, which requires only 60 steps compared to the 100 steps in 3DGStream.
elements.sent-2411-428: Moreover, the selective inheritance stage is highly lightweight, as it optimizes only one learnable parameter for each Gaussian primitive, in contrast to the tens of parameters required in a fully tuned pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-429: In addition, our dynamics-aware shift stage assigns different deformation fields for dynamic and static components, focusing on significant and subtle variations, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-430: This strategy enhances the convergence and achieves high-quality deformations with fewer optimization steps (100 steps instead of 150 steps in 3DGStream).
elements.sent-2411-431: Although our pipeline requires optical flow and segmentation, these results are either directly inferred or rendered within milliseconds and are executed only once every few timesteps.
elements.sent-2411-432: Beyond the significant efficiency advantage, our reconstruction fidelity also outperforms previous online streamable baselines~\cite{li2022streaming,sun20243dgstream,gao2025hicom and surpasses most state-of-the-art offline training methods~\cite{li2022neural,song2023nerfplayer,cao2023hexplane,fridovich2023k,attal2023hyperreel,wang2023mixed,wu20244d,bae2024per .}}
